title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] YURIKO HIROTA: Hi, I'm
Yuriko, the web ecosystem consultant at Google. SWETHA GOPALAKRISHNAN: And I'm
Swetha, also a web ecosystem consultant at Google. YURIKO HIROTA: We've all seen
the flashy demos, the Hello World examples that
promises seamless AI integrations on websites. But let's be honest, while the
Hello World examples give us the fancy glimpse of
what could be done, they are still prototypes. They don't really show us how
AI can actually be integrated in the real world websites. But now get ready to
look beyond the hype because the future of AI
and web isn't just theory. It's already happening. Today we're going to skip
the theoreticals and what-ifs and get straight
to the real deal-- checking out actual AI feature
examples on the websites and breaking down
the tech behind them. In this talk, we will first walk
you through the potential ways to implement the AI
features to your websites. And we will showcase
the real world use cases to inspire what
powerful AI features you can build on the web. We will also pull
back the curtain and give you an overview of
the technical architectures and APIs that enable these
amazing real world use cases. Sounds fun. Are you ready? Let's dive in. First, let's recap
on the three ways you can implement AI
features on your websites. So the first way is using
the server-side models like Vertex AI. This is great for running large
models for complex use cases or training and retraining
models on your data. Then what many people
might not realize is that you also
have another arrow in your quiver of AI toolkit. That is running AI
on the client side. The benefit of running AI on
the client side is threefold. It's performance since
there's no network dependency. It even works offline. It's private since everything
stays on your device-- your prompt, the data
you sent with the prompt, and the response. Last but not least,
it's cost efficient. Once the browser
downloads the model, running AI is free of cost. You can think of running
AI on the server-side
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
your prompt, the data
you sent with the prompt, and the response. Last but not least,
it's cost efficient. Once the browser
downloads the model, running AI is free of cost. You can think of running
AI on the server-side as cooking in one of
those big chef's kitchens. There's a lot of resources
like huge countertops, and huge refrigerators, and
many chefs just running around. On the other hand, running
AI on the client-side, it's like cooking in
your home kitchen. You don't have as vast
resources as a chef's kitchen, but it's not costly
to implement. You have a basic
toolkit and ingredients. Enough to meet your needs. In addition, the home
kitchen is private so you can do any kind
of crazy cooking you want and no one will notice. Now you might want to use the
client-side models for privacy, performance, and
cost efficiencies. However, some AI
tasks might need to be complemented with
the server-side models. And that's where the
hybrid approach comes in. One of the reasons for
using a hybrid approach is because of the
model capability. For example, let's
say a user wants to ask a question
on an AI chatbot about a service
you're providing. With the client-side
AI, it might be able to answer the
question if the model has sufficient data. However, if the model is not
able to provide the answers, then you can fall back
to the server-side, which can be trained on a more
complex and larger data set. Another reason for using
the hybrid approach is the device capability. Let's say you want
to make your AI features accessible to
everyone on your platform but your users might be
using a variety of devices. In such cases, you
might want to decide whether to run the
model depending on the environmental factors. For example, if
the user is offline but has a cached
model on the browser, run AI on the client-side. Or if the user is on a low end
device which cannot run any AI on the client-side, then
fall back to the server side. Later in the talk, we will show
how real world websites utilize
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
run AI on the client-side. Or if the user is on a low end
device which cannot run any AI on the client-side, then
fall back to the server side. Later in the talk, we will show
how real world websites utilize the hybrid approaches to
make AI inference more efficient so stay tuned. Now covering the server-side,
and the client-side, and the hybrid solution
in this talk is a lot. Fortunately, if you're
looking for more information on the server-side solutions,
our Google Cloud colleagues provide many amazing resources. There's a link in
the description so go check them out. For the rest of this
talk, we will specifically focus on the two solutions,
client-side and hybrid. Now before diving into
the real world examples, you might want to
familiarize yourself with the set of technologies
that power these examples. First, you got
your low level APIs like WebGPU and WebAssembly
or WASM for short. Second, you got your
high level libraries that wrap these low
level APIs and make your lives a hundred times
easier to utilize the AI models. These libraries include
MediaPipe and Transformers.js. Last but not least,
there are some browser built-in models and APIs. These are already set up for
you to use out of the box so all you need is to
plug-in to a JavaScript API. Chrome has a set of APIs
built into the browser called the built-in AI APIs. If you want to learn more, refer
to the talk in the description. Now I will pass the baton over
to my amazing colleague Swetha, who will walk you through the
real world examples of websites that implement seamless AI
and achieved critical business impact. Over to you, Swetha. SWETHA GOPALAKRISHNAN:
Thanks, Yuriko. It's very much possible
to leverage the strategies we just mentioned
in your own website. Let's see how some
companies did this. First, let's look at some
client-side use cases. Bilibili is an entertainment
content platform with over 330 million
monthly active users. To make their live video
streams more engaging, they created a feature called
bullet screen comments, which
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
First, let's look at some
client-side use cases. Bilibili is an entertainment
content platform with over 330 million
monthly active users. To make their live video
streams more engaging, they created a feature called
bullet screen comments, which displays real time user
comments over the video. The challenge Bilibili
faced was that the comments can become disruptive
if they are rendered on top of the speaker. So they needed a way to know
which areas of the screen are the speaker and
which ones aren't so that they could render the
comments behind the speaker. Fortunately, this is a well
understood problem in the AI field called image segmentation
and has out of the box models available. It works by extracting
the speaker's outline from the image,
enabling developers to know which parts to modify. In Bilibili's case, they
could use this model to render user comments
behind the speaker. But given that they serve a huge
amount of content every day, they needed a solution that
was low cost, low latency, and accurate. To achieve this,
they used MediaPipe and tensorflow.js's
selfie segmentation model, running fully client-side. And since the model leverages
WebAssembly and the device's GPU under the hood, it allows the
algorithm to run in real time with very low latency, a
critical factor for live video streaming. Thanks to this client-side
solution on the web, they were able to improve
the viewing experience. In fact, they saw a 30%
increase in session duration and 19% higher click-through
rate for live streaming videos. Vision Nanny, part of the Google
for Startups program in India, is a web platform
developed for children with cerebral visual
impairment, a leading cause of childhood blindness. It provides AI-powered
vision stimulation activities such as color identification,
counting, hand-eye coordination, and more. This is an example of
a hand-eye coordination activity where the child has
to drag the milk to the cat. The best part is these use
cases run fully client-side,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
such as color identification,
counting, hand-eye coordination, and more. This is an example of
a hand-eye coordination activity where the child has
to drag the milk to the cat. The best part is these use
cases run fully client-side, which enables parents and
educators to access them anywhere anytime such
as rural areas or places with poor internet. This also addresses
parents' concerns about their children's privacy,
as the child's image never leaves the device. They chose to build
for the web as it is accessible from a
wide variety of devices. Their use cases were powered
by MediaPipe's out-of-the-box libraries. This particular example
uses MediaPipe's hand landmark detection model, which
locates key points of the hands on an image, video
frame, or in real time. Another activity they provide
uses an audio classification model. In this multi-sensory
activity, children interact with onscreen
elements using squeaky toys. And they use WebGPU
when available, allowing the model
to run faster. A pilot study with 50 children
showed that Vision Nanny's web platform delivered five
times faster responses, improving dexterity,
coordination, and cognitive skills. Therapists also reported saving
three hours per therapy session by eliminating manual
setup of activities. Google Meet has a number of
features that are enabled by AI. I'm sure we've
been in video calls where there's bad lighting
and blurry videos. It's not very ideal. Right? Google Meet tackles
this with some cool AI features, Studio
Lighting and Studio Look to improve video quality. Let's start with
Studio Lighting. Imagine you're in
a dimly lit room. Meet uses AI to make
it look like you're in a professional studio. How does that work? First, a pipeline of AI
models analyzes the speaker, figuring out its shape
and light reflection. Then the browser uses your
GPU to magically relight the speaker, like
adding a virtual light. Now Studio Look. This feature
sharpens and reduces the noise of the speaker's face. A face detection model finds
the face and another model
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
Then the browser uses your
GPU to magically relight the speaker, like
adding a virtual light. Now Studio Look. This feature
sharpens and reduces the noise of the speaker's face. A face detection model finds
the face and another model enhances it, making it clearer
and reducing fuzziness. But the challenge here
was that these AI features need to work in real time. That means the browser has to
process a lot of video data and fast. JavaScript, while
powerful, sometimes isn't enough for this
kind of heavy lifting. That's where WebAssembly
or WASM comes in. Google Meet uses a powerful C++
library called MediaPipe for its video processing. Normally, C++ code is
for native applications. But now with WASM, we
can take that C++ code, compile it into a
web-friendly binary format, and run it directly in the
browser with near-native speed. This is crucial for
real-time video processing. Essentially, WASM lets us
tap into the full power of your computer's CPU, which
JavaScript alone can't always do. Thanks to WASM, Google Meet
delivers amazing AI features across all major browsers,
making us look great on camera. Without it, these real-time
AI video enhancements wouldn't be possible on the web. Beyond Studio Lighting
and Studio Look, Meet also uses a client-side
background segmentation model and conducted an experiment
comparing WebGPU and WebGL backends. Early results show up to 22%
decrease in power consumption when using WebGPU
on MacBook Air M2. This translates directly
to a longer battery life and a smoother
experience for users. On top of that, using a
brand new WebGPU feature called subgroup
broadcast, Google Meet is allowing advanced
features like background blur and replacement to run with
significantly improved power efficiency. These WebGPU-powered
features should be rolling out later this year. So far, we've looked at some
amazing client-side examples. Now let's look at how
some companies implemented a hybrid approach. Tokopedia, an Indonesian
technology company,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
These WebGPU-powered
features should be rolling out later this year. So far, we've looked at some
amazing client-side examples. Now let's look at how
some companies implemented a hybrid approach. Tokopedia, an Indonesian
technology company, is another great example that
saw significant business impact from implementing
client-side AI features. Mitra Tokopedia was one of
their web applications, which helped small business owners
in more than 700 cities to sell digital products. A key step in Mitra
Tokopedia's onboarding process required these sellers
to verify their identity by uploading their national
ID and a selfie with the ID. However, most sellers did not
upload their photos correctly in the first step, causing
the verification data to be rejected. This created thousands
of tickets per week to the operations team
for manual verification. Tokopedia was keen to solve
this problem using ML. One of their key considerations
was fast download and runtime performance for quick
feedback to users and to cater to a variety of devices. They used MediaPipe and
TensorFlow's face detection library, a package that provides
models for running real time face detection. Specifically, the
tensorflow.js runtime was used. The model returns a
list of faces detected. The result contains a bounding
box of each detected face as well has an array of six key
points for the detected face. This includes features such
as the eyes, nose, and mouth. By adding this model
at the very first step of the onboarding
process, they were able to detect the
seller's face clearly. Upon successful
verification by the model, the relevant seller information
was passed to the backend. If the verification
failed, the seller was given a failure message
and an option to retry. It's worth noting that they
implemented a hybrid approach where the face detection
model ran client-side if the device could support it. Otherwise, it was
run on the server. This allowed them to achieve
fast feedback to users while catering to older
or low end devices.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
where the face detection
model ran client-side if the device could support it. Otherwise, it was
run on the server. This allowed them to achieve
fast feedback to users while catering to older
or low end devices. As a result of this
implementation, the rejection rate
significantly improved. To be more specific, the
number of manual approvals decreased by almost 70%. And the onboarding rejection
rate decreased by over 20%. This not only created a
smoother experience for sellers but also reduced the operational
cost for the Tokopedia team. Policy Bazaar is one of
the leading insurance platforms in India with 97
million registered customers. They noticed that a
significant number of users were coming onto their
platform in the evenings after the customer support
team's working hours and wanted to provide better
service to those users. To achieve this,
Policy Bazaar built a text and voice-powered
insurance assistance chatbot called Finova AI that combines
multiple AI models using a hybrid architecture where
some parts of the solution run client-side and
some on the server side. Here's how their solution works. The user input goes through the
built-in language detector API. And if it's an
Indic language, it gets converted to English using
the built-in translator API. To learn more about
these built-in APIs, refer to the talk
mentioned on this slide. The translated query is then
passed to a server-side model trained on Policy Bazaar's data
and returns an English response to the user's question. The translator API then
converts the text back into the user's native
language if required. Policy Bazaar's example
shows us that you don't need to choose
between client side and server-side models. You can combine or
chain multiple models by catering each model
to a specific task in the overall flow. Where low latency, cost,
and privacy was required, a client-side solution was used. Where more complex models
trained on custom data was needed, a server-side
solution was used. At the end of this
customer conversation,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
Where low latency, cost,
and privacy was required, a client-side solution was used. Where more complex models
trained on custom data was needed, a server-side
solution was used. At the end of this
customer conversation, a transcription is forwarded
to the human support staff for follow up. However, what happens
if a user decides to provide inappropriate or
aggressive input to the chatbot? To tackle this, they used
a toxicity detection model that runs fully client-side
and powered by WebGPU. Why client-side
toxicity detection? Due to privacy and
sensitivity reasons, they wanted to analyze
messages on the device and only send meaningful
data to the server. And eventually, when
the conversation is sent to human
agents for follow-ups, they wanted to avoid
toxic language. This was the
motivation to implement a client-side solution. Let's see this in action. When the toxicity
detection model identifies a toxic
message, the user is prompted to
rephrase the message. The message is not
sent to the server. An added benefit was that
when they switched from WebGL to WebGPU backend for supported
browsers, the model ran 10 times faster, which meant faster
feedback to the user to revise their message. So far, we've seen
how AI is making a real difference in the web
experiences we use every day. Now let's pull back
the curtain and explore the technologies that made
these features possible. Over to you, Yuriko. YURIKO HIROTA: All right. Let's dive in. For the past few
years, Chrome has been building primitives
like WebGPU and WASM that allows AI to
run on the web. As a developer,
though, you'll probably interact with AI
via handy libraries like MediaPipe and
transformer.js. These libraries abstract
away the complexities, allowing you to focus
on building amazing AI features for your websites. Let's briefly walk through how
you can utilize these libraries in your websites. If you're interested in creating
similar features like Bilibili, then you can use MediaPipe's
Image Segmenter Tasks.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – YN04nNsg_L4
text:
features for your websites. Let's briefly walk through how
you can utilize these libraries in your websites. If you're interested in creating
similar features like Bilibili, then you can use MediaPipe's
Image Segmenter Tasks. This task uses WebAssembly
to let you segment images into regions based on the
predefined categories. This allows for
the identification of specific objects or textures,
such as a person, a background, or a cat. Also, did you know
that MediaPipe lets you run large language
models completely on the browser thanks to WebGPU? The LLMs that run
on the browsers are typically smaller
and less capable than the server-side
LLMs, but they're good for scoped tasks such
as generating small texts, retrieving information
in natural language form, or summarizing documents. You also have transformer.js,
a library closely associated with the growing Hugging
Face ecosystem that lets you use many
of the ready-made AI models on the website
for different tasks, including segmentation
tasks, classification tasks, speech recognitions, and more. The addition of WebGPU support
in transformers.js version three last year has generated
excitement and creativity amongst the developers. Here are the
snippets of examples that would not
have been possible without the support of WebGPU. We've seen developers
experimenting with reasoning models like DeepSeek optimized
for in-browser inference entirely on Chrome. On top of that, Kokoro, an
open way text-to-speech model with 82 million
parameters, can now be used to convert text
to natural sounding speech and runs in-browser
thanks to transformer.js. It's 100% free and supports
unlimited generations. Running a multimodal
vision language model called Janus Pro One Billion was
also made possible on the web, meaning you can create high
quality images from text descriptions and engage in
detailed image discussion, for instance. Developers' enthusiasm and
creativity are just incredible. Since Chrome has been at the
forefront of AI technologies,

meaning you can create high
quality images from text descriptions and engage in
detailed image discussion, for instance. Developers' enthusiasm and
creativity are just incredible. Since Chrome has been at the
forefront of AI technologies, WebGPU is well supported
in Chrome browser. It is also available in Safari
Tech Preview and Firefox Nightly as we're recording. Luckily for us, WebAssembly's
broad support across browsers, positions the web as a platform
ready for AI development, accessible to all users. I hope that gave you a
glimpse into the technologies available to you today. Let me hand it back to
Swetha to wrap it up. SWETHA GOPALAKRISHNAN:
So far, we've looked at strategies,
real world examples, and the underlying technologies
for you to build AI on the web. If you want to learn more,
check out the link on the slide. I hope this talk inspired
you to think of what you can develop for your own web app. We can't wait to see
what you'll build. Thank you. [MUSIC PLAYING]
